item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that it's a moving cloud. When people sleep under it, they have a weird dream of flying up to the cloud and entering a dream-space. Sap harvested from it gives people the ability to fulfill other peoples dreams. It was created by some strange dream-harvester guy who wants people to not fulfill their dreams.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting idea, but I'm not sure I understand a) what exactly the scip does and b) why it warrants a Keter. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The 4000 Contest ended months ago, how am I just finding this gem now?? Great plot, writing, everything. Incredibly well done!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the concept of a sky-island which lures in lucid dreamers and literally eats their hopes and dreams, but it was too neatly explained and the whole interview with SCP-4321-P felt too much like a Hollywood villain monologue. The superpower sap also seemed really disconnected from the rest of the article. I get why so many people love this article tho, it's very detailed and distinct, but not as much my thing wrt the execution.

item.title: Re: Edit Made
item.content:encoded: Asher457, you're replying to a comment that's over 6 months old, with a relatively contentless post. Maybe check timestamps from now on before posting.

item.title: Re: Edit Made
item.content:encoded: Magic!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of the SCPs that frustrates me because I just… How did the first girl on the cloud get there? There are so many questions that I still have and it's something that I can enjoy, when an SCP actually makes me want more.

It’s funny, after writing this I’ve gotten a strange nostalgic feeling whenever I look at a cloud.

Thanks for +200 guys.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wholesome. +1 and my love to this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
JuSt fOlLoW YoUr hEaRt, MaN
No, but seriously, folks.
I didn't really care for that part (it fundamentally changes everything we know about thaumaturgy in general and is just cliche, and didn't seem to possess much relevance to a dream-eating cloud) but the rest was interesting.
In particular, it implies that the cloud used to be a human… or whatever existed before humans. Before homo sapiens. And it was a proto-human, incredibly powerful reality bender. Possibly the most powerful there's ever been.
It used the power of its own dreams to transform it into an immortal state, where it absorbs dreams and gives them out in its spare time.
Honestly, I thought the cloud was Mordred, the folkloric sorcerer, for a second- all that thaumaturgy had to be going somewhere, right?
But +1 as it stands.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And it explains why NZ is missing on so many maps…. :O Wish I could double upvote just for that!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes! These are all NZ birds on purpose. NZ has a lot of different flightless birds, and well… this island was once on the ground somewhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP could be described as "the land of the long white cloud", and the bones recovered are extinct NZ species, if my memory serves me correctly- are these references to NZ intentional? Big high five if they are.

item.title: Series 5 liftoff of 4... 3... 2... 1.
item.content:encoded: You know, writing SCPs is hard, but it’s worth all the effort to know you entertained someone in the end. Thank you all for reading! And just remember, when life’s feeling heavy, and everything around you feels like an emergency, just take a look up at the sky and make yourself, and your problems, feel small.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As others have pointed out, there were points in this that were really quite good, and others that were just kind of meh. For example, the character interactions are great-you do a solid job of fleshing out characters with realistic interactions and dialogue.

Ultimately though, the central story didn't quite grip me as it seemed to have grabbed other people. You play around a lot with the concept of people losing their dreams and how that affects them, but do very little to address the other half of the story (gaining powers) aside from a fairly bland experiment log.

It's a well-written piece, so it's not a downvote from me, but not quite enough for the upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the way you spun out the narrative, I love the basic concept, and I love the title.

The sap feels a bit too powerful, but overall I absolutely adored this, excellent job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of the saddest skips I've read. If there's something more gripping than a skip that will keep you awake for days, it's a skip that will put you down in the dumps.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this. A floating island that steals people's willpower via eating their dreams in order to increase it's own magical power is a pretty unique and interesting concept, and the character writing is superb.

My only complaint is that it kind of seems like the Foundation could use it to easily produce super soldiers? I mean, no negative side effects were mentioned for the test subjects who ate the sap and developed magical superpowers themed after their own dreams. Perhaps a downside should be added; for example, maybe the island will specifically target people who eat the sap in order to take it's stolen willpower back, so those empowered by the sap will soon become victims of the island and lose them, or in the case of lucid dreamers, they suffer from sleep deprivation from constantly having to evade the island in their dreams.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I loved this.

I have a suggestion for after the contest. Moose joined the Foundation based on a dream, and was driven to become the 19 director based on it. Seems too perfect an opportunity to miss. Mind if I run some material past you?

item.title: Re: Upvoted, but now I just feel I'll never have these feelings again.
item.content:encoded:
Various types of responses
REEEEEAAAAACCCHH

So, first, the satellite imaging was actually a result of someone noting that as something the Foundation would take care of, just so NASA or someone wouldn't find it. It's one of those minor details that's kinda hit or miss with people.

I tend not to give explicit dimensions more for narrative purposes. I prefer to let the reader image how large the island is themselves, rather than limit it. Sure, the Foundation would probably have the dimensions, but it's not super important to the article, so I'll flex my poetic license a little.

It was either going to be radar or sonar. Just sorta chose one. Might flip it to radar later.

Good catch on the repeat, I've fixed it now.

I will PM you at some point with a more detailed description of the Copperfield Cages. I'm excited to see how a blue print would look!

KIRBY MY POOR HEART JUST CAN'T TAKE THIS STOP PLAYING WITH ME

Someone once told me writing pathos based pieces is like being a puppeteer, making the reader dance by pulling just right on the heart strings. I see I've done my job :D

item.title: Upvoted, but now I just feel I'll never have these feelings again.
item.content:encoded:
My opinion.
KIIIIIIIIRBYYYYYYYY

Satellite imaging of the earth is to be intercepted and edited to remove appearances of SCP-4000.

Breaks my credibility a bit, but then again, it might be my subconscious envy because you wrote a floating continent.

immense landmass

Can't we narrow its dimensions a bit? This is a floating continent, it must have somewhat constant dimensions and if it doesn't it'll not detract me from stating that my subconscious envy is due to your writing a floating continent as a skip.

Sonar later confirmed that cloud contained SCP-4000's landmass.

Not radar? I ask simply because it sounds like the way you would detect floating solid objects; it's a landmass, you would expect radar to detect something so large as a floating continent, or as cool, and I say that with subconscious envy since you wrote a floating continent as a skip.

50 Foundation personnel trained in lucid dreaming gathered in an uninhabited area intercepting SCP-4000's projected flight path. A separate team of researchers operated Neuron Imaging Engines (NIEs) designed to detect sensory information during REM sleep and convert it into a video format through the use of MRI technology and thaumaturgy. However, due to the abstract nature of dreams, participants were directed to narrate their experience to compare with the detected visuals.

Nothing wrong with this, I just had to mention that adding this bit makes this floating continent SCP article you wrote and about which I feel tremendous, perfectly repressed envy, even better.

No it's not that. No, it's not that.

Is this intentionally repeated? In any case, it is a part of an awesome article about a floating dream continent, so it might be just the envy.

A Copperfield Cage

I simply have to make a blueprint of this. I beg you contact me via wikidot PM to send me specifications, to which I'll respond that you made an article about a floating dream continent.

Well, it was nice working with you.

KIRBY MY POOR HEART JUST CAN'T TAKE THIS STOP PLAYING WITH ME

This was exactly what it could be. I loved every step. Historyish? Not much, but still a damn great skip. Congratulations, and have an upvote.

item.title: Re: Title Change
item.content:encoded: It’s also just a damn beautiful thought that I appreciate regularly.

item.title: Re: Title Change
item.content:encoded: Originally I had "When Dreams Come True" to play off of the motif of the realization of one's desires. However, I realized that wasn't really thematically similar to what the article is about, so I decided to go with the current title which is more meandering, which fits more with the tone I was going for.

item.title: Re: Title Change
item.content:encoded: What was the original title?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad you enjoyed! And good catch on the typos, those should be fixed now!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed reading this. +1.
But I noticed a couple little things. The second "to" in this sentence is probably meant to be "so":

Due to the ways in which the dreams we've seen to far tend to depict deviations from our understanding of history…

I think you left behind a word here which you meant to delete:

She jumps off the stairs into the hole, and begins to accelerate, reaching moving faster than terminal velocity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No no, I remember the entire conversation, heh. I just didn't pick up on any other links to those skeletons throughout the rest of the article. It's entirely possible that I just missed them, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad you enjoyed! I'm happy the tone worked, since I felt like it needed to be somewhat subdued to parallel Pine's character arc. And I'll go fix that little mistake.

Also…

the result is that the one change I did notice doesn't seem to actually go anywhere and feels a little tacked on (the bird skeletons).

spoilersish
– hide block

So, the bird skeletons are supposed to be there to imply that the island is flying/was originally created by the collective will of a number of flightless birds. The placement seems a little strange, but that's because I don't have any direct callbacks. The connections is supposed to hit as fridge logic during the interview with "dream-Pine", when he discusses how "the ground felt wrong".

(Funny enough, I believe I brainstormed this idea with you, but I don't blame you for forgetting :P)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Having read the draft of this, it's a little tricky to separate this from the version I read earlier in my head, but I feel like very little has changed from that draft I read. That's not bad, it was/is fine and I barely remember most of the notes I gave you, but the result is that the one change I did notice doesn't seem to actually go anywhere and feels a little tacked on (the bird skeletons).

Ultimately though, I do like this. I enjoyed the fairly sedate pace and tone of the thing, and the fact that it's just still doing its thing by the end, going about its business largely uninterrupted without some grand escalation or radical shift in behaviour clinches it for me. Calling the researcher Pine just made me think of this though :p
+1

One error I spotted
– hide block

Like, I little glints of thought are entering my mind.

Looks like the "I" here is an extra word, or maybe another word is missing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Lucid dreaming is such a cool concept and you did it justice. I really liked the hook about siphoning willpower too. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad you enjoyed! And yeah, I do see what you mean about the different experiments thing, if I think of something I'll fix it, but right now anything that comes to mind requires more than a little restructuring.

I had the worst visceral reaction to the Canucks winning the Cup, and I'm not even a Bruins fan.

Full disclosure, I don't know anything about hockey. I just uh… googled some teams.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had the worst visceral reaction to the Canucks winning the Cup, and I'm not even a Bruins fan.

That being said, the idea of just. not caring anymore speaks to me on such a personal level, so. upvote

My one thing is kinda nitpicky and I'm not sure if there even is a way to fix it, but having references to previous tests/other outside research in notes that are specific to one test seems a bit unprofessional to me? I dunno if this makes any sense but (at least to me) notes written down under a single test should refer to that test and its results only. In terms of storytelling, though, I'm not sure if there's a way to change this and keep what you've got right now, so it's whatever.

Yay! While this might eat dreams, at least it let my little one of hitting triple digits come true! Thanks for reading!

Also, I forgot to make this update earlier but thanks to Shaggydredlocks for making a few suggestions to help the article read more cleanly.

So… hooray! Thanks again everyone!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We've got all kind of monsters, maniacs, eldritch horrors, doomsday artifacts, and accursed knowledge locked up in here.

And I honestly have to say that this might honestly be the cruelest and most evil skip yet.

Just, damn. :(

(hell yes you get an upvote)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good shit. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This skip definitely has the foundations for a good work, but overall, I feel like it leaves nothing for the reader to think about besides how history could have played out differently, which is already a trope. It also gives too much away over a frankly stretched article, and I found that disappointing and not particularly interesting. In addition, the whole piece about the purple goo giving people powers seems tacked-on and superfluous to what the article needs to be interesting.

I only downvote in the worst of circumstances, so this will receive a novote from me. Best of luck with this!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This loss of motivation and giving into being just another number hits incredibly home for me. I would rather it be some outside force that sapped my will and not just me, take this sad upvote lol.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I worry a lot about whether or not I'll ever do anything significant with my life, so this article hit a deep-seated fear in me. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article made me sad, but rightfully so. Great work on the entry!
+1.

I weep not for my present, but the version of me who relishes in the gift that is his.

:(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That Canucks experiment was a little too real for me.

+1.

item.title: Edit Made
item.content:encoded: Alright, due to the number of comments I've gotten about the NIEs, I've included a brief mentioning of thaumaturgy to help suspend disbelief.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
'These neural patterns resemble more tree-thought patterns as opposed to face-thought patterns' (those studies look pretty dubious from a quick skim - 3 participants?) versus 'we have translated your brain waves into understandable and accurate reproductions of what you are dream-seeing and the speech you are hearing' have such a vast gulf between them they may as well be completely unrelated. It's like saying that since we got to the moon, it's plausible to think FTL travel is around the corner with slightly more advanced current technology.

I don't think completely accurate dream-recording is a deal breaker for my suspension of disbelief in the Foundation universe, but it does just need to be handwaved away with magic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's an article in Discover Magazine that points to some studies where they say they currently they can't do sound, but they can do low resolution pictures. If you dreamed of a face vs a tree for example, the computer can define that.

No reason to think the Foundation can at least do medium resolution pictures and low quality sound.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the concept, but it seemed to go off the rails a bit on the physical exploration log. I wasn't sure what it meant by a pool of liquid "feeling conscious" or "dreaming bigger than us", which threw me and I wasn't able to finish reading.

Not sure about this competition but if those bits were worked on a bit this could be a very good skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good catch, in my head he had rewatched the video before going up but… yeah I'll make some edits to clean that up.

Also re:

purple goo that turns people into superheroes.

spoilers, sorta
also me explaining my writing because I like to do that

So, I mean I guess you could call it clumsy, but it ties into two aspects of the anomaly that both relate to the core themes. First of all, I base it around sap, because the island "saps their motivation" (yeah, it's a play on words, but I thought it was clever enough to run with).

Secondly, and more importantly, it's supposed to tie into the question of why the hell the island is flying in the first place. It's needed to demonstrate the connection between the dreams and magic before I put it into more clear terms, and also it sets up the table at the end of the 3rd collapsible. If I had left it out, a lot of the "flight" motif would be gone, and I would have no reason for why the island is flying.

Also, the experiment log is my main demonstration of how this connects to history, and without it I probably wouldn't consider this part of the theme myself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
During subsequent questioning about the dream, Pine could only recall the sensation of flight, although he described this sensation as "mild" and "dulled".

Hart: Alright, thanks for the update. [to Pine] You remember how far it is until the pool place?

… ?

Bigger point, there was a lot going on here, with some character interactions and imagery that I like, and with some other things that don't make a lot of sense. Biggest thing is that you weighed down a lot of an SCP about dreams and ambition and weird fey-creature logic with… purple goo that turns people into superheroes. It's just so discordant and there's no reason to go there, it's just a clumsy overly-literal metaphor.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for the feedback!

the Foundation talk so casually about magic as this easily understood, quantifiable thing

So, the quantifiability of magic was actually based off Clef's piece on thaumaturgy, and the casual tone, assuming you're referring to Moose's interview, was used by design to demonstrate what it looked like after someone had undergone an AVIATION event, laid back, not a care in the world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno, this just felt very cheesy to me and it left a bad taste in my mouth to see the Foundation talk so casually about magic as this easily understood, quantifiable thing. I'm also seconding TL in that the dream recorders are just too convenient.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad you enjoyed!

I will say that I'm not sure the exit interview is necessary for the article.

Just because I'll take any excuse to explain my own writing, I wanted to keep this because it was always set to be the emotional climax for the piece. Like, sure it doesn't directly relate to the anomaly, but I could justify it being there, and I felt like it was needed to wrap up Pine's character arc.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this. I think the best part of it is that Pine's unwillingness to continue is foreshadowed in the experiment logs very subtlely.

Or maybe the best part is Dream Pine showing off the collection, perfectly explaining what makes success look so good. It's the happiness of overcoming something difficult and new.

Or maybe the best part is the last interview log, because holy crap that is sad and I wasn't expecting this to be a sad story.

Or maybe this is just my favorite thing right now. +1

EDIT: I realize all the things I mentioned are Pine centric, but he is a pretty good character focus for this article. His journey is what makes this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That final interview is so sad… And my name is Holly so it caught me off guard :p

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: didn't mean to reply, sorry

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wasn't sure where it was going at first but I think the whole thing wraps up nicely. I will say that I'm not sure the exit interview is necessary for the article. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I loved it as a draft and love it here.

The alternative histories you provide glimpses of are interesting, and honestly, I wish there were more entries logged. Additionally, you make the embodiment of SCP-4000 come across as sinister while not being overly cartoony.

+1. Job well done, CK.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know much about voting on each other's 4000 entries, but it seems to be allowed. I upvoted when I read "A disinformation campaign is underway to convince the general population that dreams of flight are common occurrences."

item.title: Title Change
item.content:encoded: So this morning I thought of a better title. Thanks 19 for confirming that it was, in fact, an improvement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Floaty dream island. Gud stuf. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How does that even work? Dreams are so personalized, they can't possibly find a universal language of brain waves and muscles twitches to translate into cohesive imagery. Talking the real, here; if it's conceptualized in our world, there's no doubt the Foundation could have a version that actually works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, one of the main driving themes I was going for, (most prominent during the experiment log and the first part of the 3rd collapsible) is that history is dictated as much by the events that don’t occur as the events that do. So many people have potential to change the world, yet only so many actually wind up in the history books.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like what you're doing with the magical element. Connecting the "willpower" and the "magic" is a great concept for a story and philosophically inspiring to boot.

But what was the connection to history?
